briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 13, 2026. Based on the climate-related news collected today, we aim to provide an in-depth analysis of domestic and international climate change trends and our efforts to address them.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening International Cooperation and Domestic Climate Response Efforts
sections.content: Today, Korea signed a Framework Agreement on Climate Change Cooperation with Georgia, laying the groundwork for international reduction projects to achieve national greenhouse gas reduction targets and securing its first foothold in the Caucasus region [1, 2, 3, 7, 9, 10, 15]. This move is particularly significant as it comes amidst UN experts supporting a resolution affirming member states' obligations to address climate change [43]. Domestic climate change response company EWC is also participating in global climate action by signing climate business agreements with companies in Ghana and Kenya [6]. Meanwhile, domestically, Yeosu City is accelerating preparations for an international conference 40 days ahead of hosting the UNFCCC Climate Week, thereby raising awareness of climate change response [5, 8, 13].

sections.title: ⚠️ Global Warnings of a Deepening Climate Crisis
sections.content: The alarm bells of the climate crisis are ringing globally. Recent results from 10,000 simulations have calculated a massive global damage cost of 280,000 won per ton of greenhouse gas, emphasizing the ripple effects of climate change on the global economy [4, 11, 12]. In particular, scientists' warnings that Austrian glaciers are collapsing at an unprecedented rate due to climate change are causing serious concern [41, 58, 61, 65]. Furthermore, complex global crises are materializing, such as sandstorms from the Sahara Desert covering European skies, leading to 'red rain' [14], and reports that climate change and war are severely threatening Iran's water supply [48, 69]. Scientific findings that Earth's day is lengthening at an unprecedented rate are also further raising awareness of this crisis [45].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts to implement carbon neutrality and accelerate the transition to renewable energy are gaining momentum. Community-level participation is active, with Anseong Saemaul Undong Association holding a 'Green Anseong Cleanup' inauguration ceremony [17], and Seongbuk-gu selecting 'Carbon Neutrality Practice Guides' to encourage carbon neutrality efforts in each district [20]. Cheongju City and Daegu City are recruiting participants for the automobile carbon neutrality point system, offering incentives of up to 100,000 won for reducing mileage [21, 25, 28, 30]. In Siheung, the 'Gyeonggi Sunlight Bicycle Path,' which runs under solar panel shade, has been completed, expanding eco-friendly transportation infrastructure [24]. In the industrial sector, the transition to decarbonized renewable energy is also active, with the Korea Chemical Industry Association holding a carbon neutrality strategy and technology exchange [18], and the Korea Industrial Complex Corporation promoting the 'Industrial Complex Solar Power Support Project' [32]. Furthermore, industrial daytime electricity rates have been lowered due to a renewable energy-focused electricity tariff reform [31, 33]. However, challenges that need to be addressed are also highlighted, such as cases where SMEs participating in carbon neutrality research face financial difficulties described as a '30 billion won bomb' [16, 29], and transmission grid conflicts in the era of renewable energy [39].

sections.title: 🌍 Raising Climate Change Awareness and Exploring Adaptation Strategies
sections.content: Raising social awareness of climate change and exploring adaptation strategies are also actively underway. Nearly one-third of the 2026 Oscar nominees mentioned climate change, demonstrating the influence of popular culture [46], and the State Board of Regents has decided to implement climate change education to raise awareness among future generations [50]. A lecture series on the health impacts of climate change has been announced [52], and specific adaptation strategies are being proposed, such as ways to help gardens withstand climate change [53] or solving the climate crisis through 'light-soil' technology for underground irrigation and enhanced water efficiency [60]. Scientists are also actively researching ecosystem adaptation by deciphering strategies animals use to survive climate change [62]. However, news that the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is shifting towards downplaying the importance of climate change in its programs for water utilities [55], and that climate change-related lawsuits against oil companies in Louisiana may become more difficult [51], raises concerns about policy reversals and suggests the need for continuous monitoring and response [57].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing covered a wide range of news, from advancements in international cooperation and warnings of a deepening climate crisis, to various domestic carbon neutrality efforts, and the enhancement of social awareness regarding climate change. We must strengthen international solidarity and domestic actions to overcome the climate crisis, while simultaneously developing our adaptive capacity to a changing environment. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Experts warn of ‘attack on Americans’ lungs’ from cuts to health programs, environmental rollbacks and other plansDonald Trump’s policies are likely to drive soaring rates of lung disease and premature death, according to a wide-ranging new study by pulmonary specialists and public health experts.The analysis, published in the American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, examines policies adopted during Trump’s second term across 10 areas, including healthcare access, environmenta

summary.phenomenon: Not mentioned in the article
summary.outlook: Donald Trump's policies, including cuts to health programs and environmental deregulation, are projected to lead to a surge in lung disease and premature death rates. This analysis examined policies adopted during Trump's second term across 10 areas.
